name: Bearings — Directions by Angle
whatIsIt: A BEARING is a direction given as an angle, always measured CLOCKWISE from NORTH, and always written with THREE figures from 000° to 360°. North is 000°, East is 090°, South is 180°, West is 270°. The BACK BEARING is the direction of the return journey: add 180° if the bearing is under 180°, or subtract 180° if it is 180° or more.
whyNeeded: Ships, planes, hikers and rescue teams describe directions with bearings because 'a bit left of that hill' is useless on a map. Bearings turn direction into a precise number anyone can follow. They are standard in ICSE and IGCSE geometry.
realLifeUses: A ship's captain steering on a bearing of 075°
A pilot given a heading by air traffic control
A hiker following a compass bearing to a campsite
A rescue team reporting a location's direction from base

vocabulary.meaning: A direction measured clockwise from North, written with three figures (e.g. 060°).
The bearing for the return trip: bearing ± 180°.
Bearings always use three digits, so 40° is written 040°.

story.title: Finding the Way Back
story.text: The captain sails from the harbour on a bearing of 070° — 70° clockwise from North. Rohan asks, 'How do we get back?' 'The BACK bearing,' says the captain. 'Since 070 is under 180, we ADD 180: 070 + 180 = 250°.' They turn to 250° and head straight home. Rohan grins: 'A direction is just a clockwise angle from North — and the way back is half a turn added on.'
story.extractedProblem: The harbour to the island is a bearing of 070°. What is the back bearing (island to harbour)?

shapes.label: N at top; bearing measured clockwise from North

visual.caption: Always start at North (up) and sweep CLOCKWISE. North 000°, East 090°, South 180°, West 270°.

teachingGallery.title: The four main bearings
teachingGallery.note: Clockwise from North, in three figures.

examples.caption: North 000°, a quarter-turn clockwise is East 090°.
Half-turn is South 180°; three-quarters is West 270°.
A bearing of 060° has back bearing 060 + 180 = 240°.

standardMethod.summary: To state a bearing, measure the angle clockwise from North and write it with three figures. To find a back bearing, add 180° if the bearing is below 180°, or subtract 180° if it is 180° or more.
steps: Face North and turn CLOCKWISE to the target direction.
Read the angle and write it with three digits (e.g. 45° becomes 045°).
For the back bearing, if the bearing < 180°, add 180°.
If the bearing ≥ 180°, subtract 180° instead. The answer is always between 000° and 360°.

workedExamples.problem: Write the direction East as a three-figure bearing.
steps: East is a quarter turn clockwise from North.

workedExamples.problem: A bearing is 040°. Find the back bearing.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing a bearing as 40° instead of 040°.
commonMistakes.fix: Bearings ALWAYS use three figures, so write 040°, 090°, 007° — pad with leading zeros.

commonMistakes.mistake: Guessing the back bearing instead of using ± 180°.
commonMistakes.fix: Back bearing = bearing ± 180°: ADD 180 if the bearing is under 180°, SUBTRACT 180 if it is 180° or more.

easy.q: East as a three-figure bearing is how many degrees?

challenge.q: How many figures does a bearing always use?

questions.q: A bearing is measured which way from North?

teachBackPrompt: Use a compass or a phone compass with a grown-up. Point to something and give its bearing in three figures (measured clockwise from North). Then work out and explain the back bearing you'd follow to return.

revisionCard.summary: A bearing is a direction measured CLOCKWISE from NORTH, in THREE figures (000°–360°): N 000°, E 090°, S 180°, W 270°. Back bearing = bearing ± 180° (add if < 180°, subtract if ≥ 180°).

rememberIt.hook: Clockwise from North, three figures; back bearing = ± 180°
rememberIt.unpack: N 000, E 090, S 180, W 270. Add 180 if under 180, else subtract 180.

studentQuestions.a: So directions never get confused — 040° can't be misread as 400°, and every bearing looks the same length.

studentQuestions.q: Why does the back bearing use 180°?
studentQuestions.a: Turning around is a half-turn, and half of a full 360° circle is 180°, so the return direction is 180° away.
